Breast Cancer Gene Expression Profiling

Parisa Farrokhian

2025-04-07

1 Introduction

Breast cancer is a leading cause of cancer-related deaths worldwide, and early detection, accurate diagnosis, and effective treatment are crucial for improving patient outcomes. Gene expression data has emerged as a promising tool to enhance the prognosis and treatment of breast cancer, offering insights into the disease’s molecular mechanisms and progression.

This study analyzes gene expression data from 129 breast cancer patients, including key biomarkers like estrogen receptor (ER) and HER2, histological features such as tumor grade and lymphatic-vascular invasion (LVI), and clinical parameters like tumor size and lymph node metastasis. The research aims to determine if gene expression data can predict these clinical features and improve the diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment planning for breast cancer patients. Using statistical predictors and cross-validation, the study explores how gene expression profiles can differentiate between breast cancer subtypes and provide new biological insights.

2 Data Transformation

The phenotype data underwent a complex transformation to extract relevant clinical variables. This process involved splitting combined information into separate variables such as estrogen receptor (ER) status, HER2 status, tumor grade, tumor size, and other tumor-related features.

Selection of available clinical information.
ER HER2 B.R.grade node.status tumor.type tumor.size
C113 neg pos III pos Ductal 2.3
C114 neg neg III neg Ductal 0.9
C115 neg pos III neg Ductal 2.5
C116 neg neg III neg Ductal 3.5
C118 neg neg III pos Ductal 2.1

3 Visualisation and filtering

Exploration of the distribution of clinical variables such as ER and HER2 status has been done with visualisations. The dataset was further refined by filtering the gene expression data based on intensity and variance to eliminate genes with low expression and low variance, which helps reduce the high-dimensionality of the dataset while retaining the most informative genes.

The bar plot of tumor subtypes illustrates the distribution of breast cancer tumor subtypes based on Estrogen Receptor (ER) and HER2 status. The most common subtype is ER-positive, HER2-negative (pos_neg), followed by ER-negative, HER2-negative (neg_neg). The ER-negative, HER2-positive (neg_pos) and ER-positive, HER2-positive (pos_pos) subtypes are less frequent, with the pos_pos subtype being the least represented. This distribution suggests a predominance of ER-positive tumors in the dataset.

## 
## neg pos 
##  53  74
## 
## neg pos 
##  97  30

The bar plot of tumor types, shows the distribution of each types within the dataset. Ductal tumors are the most prevalent, with a significantly higher frequency compared to other tumor types. Lobular and Mixed tumors are much less common, each with a similar, smaller frequency. This suggests a strong dominance of ductal tumors in the study population.

## 
##  Ductal Lobular   Mixed 
##      94      19      14

The boxplot displays the distribution of tumor sizes across different tumor subtypes, categorized by estrogen receptor (ER) and HER2 status. The neg_neg subtype (both ER and HER2 negative) shows the smallest median tumor size, typically around 2 cm. The neg_pos subtype (ER negative and HER2 positive) and pos_neg subtype (ER positive and HER2 negative) exhibit similar median tumor sizes, with values ranging from 3 to 4 cm. The pos_pos subtype (both ER and HER2 positive) has a slightly larger median tumor size, around 4 cm. In all subtypes, there is a significant spread in tumor sizes, with some outliers observed, indicating variability within each group.

The histogram visualizes the distribution of tumor sizes based on estrogen receptor (ER) status. The ER-negative (red) tumors generally have a wider distribution of tumor sizes, with a higher frequency of smaller tumors, predominantly between 2 and 3 cm in size. On the other hand, ER-positive (blue) tumors exhibit a narrower distribution, with the majority falling in the 2.5 to 4.5 cm range. This suggests that ER-positive tumors tend to be more uniformly distributed around a specific size, while ER-negative tumors show more variation in size, including both smaller and larger tumors.

The stacked bar plot visualizes the proportion of Lymphatic-Vascular Invasion (LVI) status across different tumor subtypes based on ER and HER2 status. The plot shows that for each tumor subtype (neg_neg, neg_pos, pos_neg, pos_pos), a majority of the tumors are LVI negative (green bars). A small proportion of tumors in each subtype are LVI positive (blue bars), with the exception of the “neg_neg” group, where no positive LVI cases are observed. The “n” (non-classified) LVI status, indicated in red, represents a negligible proportion across all subtypes. This suggests that, overall, LVI positivity is less prevalent, and most tumors in each subtype are LVI negative.

4 Filtering

## [1] 0

4.1 I/NI calls

the I/NI (Informative/Non-Informative) calls was applied to categorize genes based on their expression levels. The variance for each gene calculated using a custom function (rowVars) that computes the variance for each row (gene) in the dataset. This was followed by the computation of the mean expression levels (geneMeans) and variance (geneVars) for each gene across all samples. The INIcalls factor was then added to the dataset, categorizing genes as either informative (I) or non-informative (NI) based on their expression variability.

Next, the proportion of informative and non-informative genes was calculated . The proportion of informative genes was visualized using a scatter plot, where informative genes (I) were represented by red dots, while non-informative genes (NI) were represented by blue dots. The plot showed the relationship between gene mean expression levels (geneMeans) and the logarithm of the variance. This allowed us to visually assess the distribution of gene expression characteristics across informative and non-informative genes. Based on the I/NI classification, a certain percentage of genes were categorized as informative, contributing more significantly to the analysis, while non-informative genes had lower expression variability and were filtered out of further analyses.

I/NI calls filters 36.2 % of the genes (6873 of the in total 18988 genes).

Mean and variance of gene expression levels, colored by I/NI calls. 

##  num [1:6601, 1:127] 6.87 8.66 10.12 7.48 8.57 ...
##  - attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2
##   ..$ : chr [1:6601] "10001_at" "10006_at" "100093630_at" "10010_at" ...
##   ..$ : chr [1:127] "C113" "C114" "C115" "C116" ...
## [1] TRUE

5 PCA + AUTO ENCODER

The image generated from the PCA (Principal Component Analysis) plot of the gene expression data shows a clear separation between the samples based on their ER (Estrogen Receptor) status. The PCA was performed on the filtered gene expression data, where the first two principal components (PC1 and PC2) were used for visualization. The plot demonstrates that samples with different ER statuses (negative and positive) are distinguishable in the first two components. The red points represent ER-negative samples, while the blue points represent ER-positive samples, highlighting the ability of PCA to reduce the dimensionality of the dataset while preserving the variance that is critical for distinguishing between these groups.

This PCA approach, combined with the subsequent autoencoder analysis, provides an efficient method to extract meaningful features from high-dimensional gene expression data, aiding in the classification and further understanding of breast cancer subtypes.

##  - attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2
##   ..$ : chr [1:6601] "10001_at" "10006_at" "100093630_at" "10010_at" ...
##   ..$ : chr [1:127] "C113" "C114" "C115" "C116" ...

6 k means

The k-means clustering was performed on the gene expression data, where five clusters were chosen based on the elbow method, which evaluates the within-cluster sum of squares (WSS) as a function of the number of clusters. The elbow method suggested that five clusters were the optimal choice, as shown by a significant drop in WSS beyond this point. A PCA plot was generated to visualize the data points with their assigned clusters, where distinct groupings were observed along the first two principal components (PC1 and PC2). Next, the distribution of tumor types across the clusters was analyzed. The results revealed that the clusters primarily consisted of Ductal tumor types, with Lobular and Mixed types appearing in smaller proportions. Furthermore, the distribution of Estrogen Receptor (ER) status across clusters was also explored, showing that most clusters contained a high proportion of negative ER status, with a few clusters exhibiting positive ER status. in addition, The visualizations clearly illustrate that focusing on a single clinical variable, such as ER status or tumor type, may not fully capture the complexity and heterogeneity of the data. Since the clustering was based on multiple features of gene expression data, the clusters reflect a more nuanced and comprehensive view of the breast cancer subtypes rather than just one clinical value. Therefore, considering multiple clinical factors together is crucial for a more accurate and holistic understanding of the data distribution.

7 Limma

In this study, differential expression analysis was conducted using linear model to identify differentially expressed genes (DEGs) between clinical groups, specifically focusing on variables for example values like ER status, HER2, and tumor size. The analysis was performed using a design matrix that incorporated clinical factors to determine which genes were significantly associated with the clinical outcomes.

To visualize the results of the differential expression analysis, two key plots were generated. The histogram of p-values provides insight into the distribution of p-values across all tested genes, with a large number of genes showing very low p-values, indicating strong evidence of differential expression. The plot reveals a sharp peak at low p-values, suggesting that many genes are significantly different between the clinical groups. This is further confirmed by the volcano plot, which plots the log fold changes against the negative log of p-values. The red points in the volcano plot indicate genes that exhibit both statistically significant p-values (below 0.05) and substantial fold changes (logFC > 2), highlighting the most important DEGs for distinguishing between the clinical conditions.

The volcano plot and histogram together provide a comprehensive view of the data, identifying a set of genes that are potentially pivotal in understanding the clinical distinctions in tumor biology. These results serve as the foundation for further use of classification tasks and feature selection for predictive modeling. The identification of key DEGs and their visualization provides an important step toward understanding the molecular underpinnings of cancer and how clinical factors influence gene expression patterns.

8 Random Forrest

A Random Forest model was developed to classify tumor types based on gene expression data, as determined from the Limma differential expression analysis. The model utilized a set of differentially expressed genes (DEGs) that were filtered based on a p-value threshold of 0.05 and a logFC threshold of 2. Hyperparameter tuning was performed using 5-fold cross-validation, focusing on the mtry parameter, which determines the number of variables randomly sampled at each split. The number of trees (ntree) was set to 50 for faster training while maintaining model robustness. The Random Forest algorithm, known for its ability to handle large datasets and reduce overfitting, provides a powerful means of classifying tumor types based on complex gene expression patterns. The model was evaluated through several performance metrics, including a confusion matrix, ROC curve, and feature importance plots.

The Feature Importance Plot is one of the key outputs of the Random Forest model. It highlights the most influential genes contributing to the model’s ability to classify tumor types accurately. Each bar represents a gene, with the length of the bar corresponding to the gene’s importance in the classification process. Longer bars indicate greater influence. By evaluating these results, we can identify genes that are strongly associated with tumor classification and, potentially, biological pathways or biomarkers relevant for therapeutic interventions. This plot provides valuable insight into the molecular basis of tumor differentiation and allows researchers to prioritize genes for further investigation. In the left plot, genes such as “X2669_at” and “X2889_at” have high importance scores, suggesting that these genes are particularly critical in distinguishing between the classes (e.g., tumor types).

The Confusion Matrix Heatmap visualizes how well the Random Forest model has classified the different tumor types. From this matrix, we see that the model correctly classified 28 samples as Ductal (true positives), but also misclassified 3 “Lobular” samples as Ductal and 2 Lobular samples as Mixed. This heatmap gives a quick overview of where the model’s classification errors are occurring, helping in understanding its strengths and weaknesses.

The ROC Curve is a diagnostic plot that evaluates the performance of the model at various thresholds. The curve for this model shows excellent performance, as it is steep, quickly reaching a high sensitivity and specificity. The plot suggests that the model is able to correctly identify the true positive class (sensitivity) while maintaining a low false positive rate (specificity). A steep curve that stays closer to the top-left corner indicates good classification performance, which is supported by this ROC curve’s sharp rise.

## Random Forest 
## 
##  90 samples
## 417 predictors
##   3 classes: 'Ductal', 'Lobular', 'Mixed' 
##

9 Go Graoh

A GO graph was generated to visualize the relationships between different biological processes enriched among the differentially expressed genes. The graph visualizes the connections between gene sets, showing their relationships and the significance of each biological process. The results suggest specific biological processes for HER2 status.
